name: Wepwawet
description: Wepwawet, also known as Upuaut, is an ancient Egyptian deity whose name translates to "Opener of the Ways." He is typically depicted as a canine, often a wolf or jackal, or as a man with a jackal's head. Wepwawet's primary function was as a guide, leading pharaohs in both life and the afterlife. He played a crucial role in military expeditions, going before the army to clear paths and ensure victory. In funerary contexts, he guided the deceased through the dangers of the underworld, ensuring safe passage to the afterlife. He was closely associated with the city of Asyut (Lycopolis in Greek), where he was the principal deity. Over time, he became associated with both Horus and Set, sometimes considered an aspect of either god, reflecting his complex and evolving role in the Egyptian pantheon. His symbols, including the standard bearing his image, were carried in processions, signifying his power and protective presence.
subtitle: Opener of the Ways

keyMyths.title: Wepwawet as Guide of the Pharaoh
keyMyths.description: Wepwawet's role as a guide for the pharaoh is central to his mythology. During military campaigns, Wepwawet was believed to precede the pharaoh's army, ensuring a clear and safe path. This divine intervention guaranteed the pharaoh's victory and solidified his position as a divinely ordained ruler. This concept extended beyond military endeavors, with Wepwawet leading the pharaoh through life's challenges and ultimately guiding him to the afterlife, ensuring his eternal reign.
keyMyths.source: Hart, George. *A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses*. Routledge, 1986.

keyMyths.title: The Opening of the Mouth Ceremony
keyMyths.description: Wepwawet was associated with the Opening of the Mouth ceremony, a ritual performed on mummies and statues to restore their senses in the afterlife. As 'Opener of the Ways,' he facilitated the reanimation process, allowing the deceased to see, hear, speak, and eat in the realm of the dead. This ceremony was crucial for ensuring the deceased's successful transition and continued existence in the afterlife, highlighting Wepwawet's importance in funerary rites.
keyMyths.source: Ikram, Salima. *Death and Burial in Ancient Egypt*. American University in Cairo Press, 2015.

keyMyths.title: Wepwawet and the Underworld
keyMyths.description: In the underworld, Wepwawet served as a protector and guide for the deceased. He navigated the treacherous landscape, warding off dangers and leading the soul to the judgment hall of Osiris. His canine form symbolized his keen senses and ability to detect hidden threats, ensuring the safe passage of the deceased through the perilous realm. His presence offered solace and assurance to the soul facing the trials of the afterlife, solidifying his role as a benevolent guardian.
keyMyths.source: Wilkinson, Richard H. *The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt*. Thames & Hudson, 2003.
